metadata.purpose: Non-woody broadleaf herbs are stored here by stable growth form. Multiple useful parts such as leaves, flowers, stalks, seeds, or roots remain represented by record fields and routes.
metadata.organization_rule: Physical file placement follows stable biological or morphological identity. Useful parts, routes, safety, and seasonal behavior remain record-level fields.

records.notes: Large wet-ground Apiaceae with purple-tinged stems and umbrella flower clusters. Correct identification is non-negotiable.
records.culinary_uses: Young stalks and some parts have old traditional use, but this is a caution-heavy edible because carrot-family confusion can go bad fast.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional aromatic medicinal plant.
records.edibility_detail: Expert-only Apiaceae entry. Treat stem/leaf use cautiously and only after full positive identification. Do not use angelica based on purple stems, white/green umbels, wet habitat, or aromatic smell alone. Treat root use as historical/traditional context, not a casual food recommendation.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Leaves / Greens only. Root use is treated as historical/traditional context, not a casual harvest lane.
records.plant_card_note: Expert-only Apiaceae green/stalk entry. Unknown wet-ground carrot-family plants stay out of the mouth; root use is not a casual harvest recommendation.

records.look_alike_notes: Angelica is a caution-heavy Apiaceae entry. Separate it from poison hemlock and water hemlock using full plant structure, stem surface/blotching, leaf and stalk traits, habitat, and root safety. Also separate it from wild parsnip, which has yellow-green umbels and sap that can cause sunlight-triggered burns.
records.risk_reason: Large wet-ground Apiaceae can be confused with deadly hemlocks if the ID is based only on white/green umbels, divided leaves, purple-toned stems, wet habitat, or aromatic smell.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Tall carrot-family plant, wet habitat, divided leaves, white/greenish umbels, and purple-toned stems can overlap with dangerous hemlock cues.
records.hard_rule: Do not use angelica or any similar wet-ground Apiaceae unless poison hemlock and water hemlock have been ruled out by full plant traits, not by smell, purple tone, or umbrella flowers alone.

lookalike_risk.label: Extreme — deadly hemlock confusion possible
lookalike_risk.summary: Angelica is a caution-heavy Apiaceae entry. Wet habitat and umbrella-like flowers make poison hemlock and water hemlock comparisons mandatory.
required_checks: Stem hair vs. smoothness check required.
Purple/red blotch check required.
Habitat moisture check required.
Root/taste checks are not substitutes for full positive identification.
Confirm Angelica structure and aromatic stalk/leaf traits; do not use purple tone alone.
Reject any unknown wetland Apiaceae root.
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Unknown wet-ground Apiaceae stays out of the mouth unless the full plant is positively identified.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Deadly poisonous — nervous-system poisoning and respiratory failure risk.
dangerous_lookalikes.warning: Deadly look-alike: purple-spotted smooth hairless stems and mousey odor are poison-hemlock stop signs.
target_traits: Confirmed Angelica structure, not just a tall Apiaceae with umbels.
Large wet-ground plant with Angelica leaf/stalk pattern and aromatic character.
No poison-hemlock-style smooth purple blotched stem pattern.
lookalike_traits: Smooth, hairless, hollow stem instead of a bristly wild-carrot stem.
Purple or reddish blotches/spots on the stem or leaf stalks.
Glossy ferny divided leaves and white umbrella-like flower clusters.
Mousey, musty, or rank odor when crushed.
Deadly poisonous; can affect the nervous and respiratory systems.
required_checks: Check smoothness and purple/red blotching pattern on stems.
Check odor and overall plant structure.
Do not identify from white umbels alone.
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Smooth purple-spotted Apiaceae stems are a do-not-eat signal.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Deadly poisonous — seizures and rapid severe poisoning risk.
dangerous_lookalikes.warning: Deadly wetland look-alike: never use or taste unknown wetland Apiaceae roots.
target_traits: Confirmed Angelica structure and safe-use context.
No thick chambered water-hemlock root crown.
Identification is based on the full plant, not wet habitat plus umbels.
lookalike_traits: Wetland, marsh, ditch, shore, or streamside habitat raises the danger level immediately.
White umbels and compound leaves with toothed leaflets can overlap with edible carrot-family plants.
Thick root crown or chambered root structure is an extreme danger sign; roots are especially hazardous.
Do not dig, taste, chew, or smell-test unknown wetland Apiaceae roots.
Deadly poisonous; rapid severe poisoning and seizures are possible.
required_checks: Check wetland root danger before any root/stalk use.
Reject chambered or thick unknown root crowns.
Do not taste wetland Apiaceae roots.
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Do not dig or taste unknown wetland Apiaceae roots.

use_links.label: Go Botany: Angelica atropurpurea
use_links.notes: Supports plant height, leaf-segment, and mericarp measurements.

field_measurements.leaf: Leaf blades 100–300 mm; final leaf segments 40–150 mm long × 40–70 mm wide
field_measurements.notes: Size source: Go Botany for Angelica atropurpurea. Apiaceae lookalike safety remains separate.

records.notes: Mint-family plant with opposite leaves, square stems, and lavender to pink shaggy flower heads.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional aromatic tea use exists, including cold-season folk use; this guide treats that as traditional context, not medical advice.
records.culinary_uses: Leaves and flowers can be used fresh or dried for aromatic culinary tea and seasoning.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Flowers, Tea / Infusions.
records.plant_card_note: Leaves and flowers can be used fresh or dried for aromatic culinary tea and seasoning. Traditional cold-season uses are separate traditional context, not medical advice.

records.source_note: Source links added for identification/range context; food-use wording remains modest and caution-framed where appropriate.

edible_use.notes: Leaves and flowers can be used fresh or dried for aromatic culinary tea and seasoning. Traditional cold-season uses are context, not medical advice.

records.edibility_detail: Culinary tea and seasoning use only; do not treat the record as medicinal dosing.

records.notes: Large biennial with huge wavy leaves, woolly undersides, deep taproot, purple thistle-like flowers, and hooked burrs. Root/stalk use is most practical before the plant becomes woody.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional-use plant; do not treat as a medical substitute.
records.culinary_uses: First-year roots can be cooked as a root vegetable; young peeled stalks/flower stalks may be cooked when tender. Avoid old woody roots and contaminated sites.
records.general_notes: Use this record for common burdock / burdock group in U.P. field use. Do not confuse the large leaves with rhubarb as a food cue; rhubarb leaves are toxic.
records.food_quality: Edible root/stalk with site and age caution

records.edibility_detail: Edible with practical caution. First-year roots are the main target; second-year roots become woody. Avoid roadside, sprayed, or contaminated soil because roots can concentrate site problems.
records.size: Large biennial rosette first year; tall flowering stalk second year.
records.taste: Earthy root vegetable when properly prepared; stalks can be mild when young.
records.plant_lane_note: Roots / Tubers for first-year roots; Leaves / Greens for young peeled stalks, with site-contamination caution.
records.plant_card_note: First-year roots and young stalks are the useful parts; avoid roadside/sprayed sites.
records.source_note: Source links added for identification/range context; food-use wording remains modest and caution-framed where appropriate.

records.notes: Delicate low weed with opposite small leaves, tiny white starry flowers, and a single line of hairs along the stem.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional cooling and soothing herb.
records.culinary_uses: Tender fresh green used in salads, sandwiches, and light cooked dishes.

records.edibility_detail: Edible and best fresh when tender.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Leaves / Greens, Flowers.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: leaf / green, stem / shoot / stalk, flower. Edible and best fresh when tender.
records.source_note: Source links added for identification/range context; food-use wording remains modest and caution-framed where appropriate.

records.notes: Roadside plant with bright blue flowers, stiff branching stems, and a basal rosette in earlier growth.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional digestive bitter and tonic herb, especially focused on the root.
records.culinary_uses: Roots are roasted for a coffee-like flavoring and young leaves can be eaten as bitter greens; flowers are sometimes used decoratively or in small amounts.

records.edibility_detail: Root and young leaves are the main uses. Leaf bitterness is real, and roots are the practical old-app copyback focus.

records.notes: This copyback is based on the old red clover entry and applied to the broader clover record in the new app. Common meadow legume with trifoliate leaves and rounded pink to purple flower heads.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional herb for tea and general tonic use.
records.culinary_uses: Flowers and young leaves are used in teas, salads, and dried food blends.
records.edibility_detail: Edible, though often more useful as tea or adjunct food than a main vegetable.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Leaves / Greens, Flowers, Tea / Infusions.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: leaf / green, flower / tea. Edible, though often more useful as tea or adjunct food than a main vegetable.

records.notes: Opposite leaves when young, smooth stems, white latex sap, and later pink flower clusters. Use only with strong confidence in identification and preparation.
records.culinary_uses: Very young shoots can be boiled in changes of water and eaten as a cooked green by people familiar with proper preparation; this is a preparation-dependent plant, not a casual raw nibble.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional medicinal use exists, but this is a plant that needs careful handling and preparation knowledge.

records.edibility_detail: Not edible raw. Traditional food use requires correct species identification and proper boiling/cooking.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Leaves / Greens.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: stem / shoot / stalk, leaf / green. Not edible raw. Traditional food use requires correct species identification and proper boiling/cooking.
records.source_note: Source links added for identification and food-context history; keep strong ID and preparation caution in this record.

records.notes: Large coarse plant with huge compound leaves, thick hollow stems, and broad white umbrella-shaped flower clusters. Sap can irritate sun-exposed skin.
records.culinary_uses: Very young peeled shoots and leaf stalks have been cooked traditionally, but this plant demands careful identification and skin protection.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional uses exist, but food use should be cautious due to look-alikes and sap sensitivity.

records.edibility_detail: Caution-heavy Apiaceae edible-use record. Use only very young peeled shoots or leaf stalks after positive identification and with gloves/skin protection; sap can irritate or burn sun-exposed skin. Unknown Apiaceae stays out of the mouth.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Leaves / Greens.
records.plant_card_note: Young peeled shoots/stalks are the practical use, but this is not a casual green. Confirm ID and use gloves/skin protection; avoid confusion with wild parsnip, hemlocks, angelica, and wild carrot.
records.look_alike_notes: Separate cow parsnip from wild parsnip, poison hemlock, water hemlock, angelica, and wild carrot. Sap/contact risk plus Apiaceae confusion makes this a caution-heavy record. Giant hogweed may be relevant as a text-only regional caution where appropriate, but no new record is added here.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat or handle cow parsnip casually. Use gloves and skin protection, and do not harvest any carrot-family plant unless poison hemlock, water hemlock, and wild parsnip have been ruled out.

field_measurements.overall: Flowering stems 4–15 ft tall / about 1.2–4.6 m
field_measurements.flower: Compound umbels 6–8 in across / about 15–20 cm
field_measurements.fruit: Oval, flattened schizocarps about 1/4 in long / about 6 mm
field_measurements.notes: Size source: NC State Extension. Apiaceae safety, sap-contact caution, and deadly lookalike checks remain separate from size.

records.notes: Common lawn and disturbed-ground perennial with a basal rosette of toothed leaves, hollow leafless flower stalks, yellow composite flowers, and wind-borne puffball seed heads.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional bitter/tonic and tea plant; this guide treats it mainly as a food and tea plant, not as medical advice.
records.culinary_uses: Leaves are used as bitter greens, flowers for fritters/tea/wine-style preparations, and roasted roots for a coffee-like drink.
records.plant_lane_note: Leaves, flowers, and roots are all useful; the most beginner-friendly uses are young greens and flowers.
records.plant_card_note: Leaves are bitter greens, flowers can be used for fritters or tea, and roasted roots can make a coffee-like drink. Avoid sprayed lawns.
records.edibility_detail: Leaves, flowers, and roots are edible from clean, unsprayed sites. Older leaves and green flower parts can be bitter.

records.notes: Orange-flowered ornamental escape with long strap-like leaves arising in fans.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly culinary ornamental escape.
records.culinary_uses: Young shoots, flower buds, and some other parts are edible in established daylily traditions.

records.notes: Biennial with basal rosette first year and tall flowering spike with yellow flowers the second.
records.culinary_uses: Root has traditional edible use, and young leaves or shoots may also be used cautiously in small amounts.
records.medicinal_uses: Well known from medicinal contexts, though here it is being tracked mainly for edible completeness.

records.notes: Tall plant of burns, clearings, roadsides, and sunny edges. Leaves alternate, narrow, and pointed. Showy pink flowers in summer.
records.culinary_uses: Young shoots are eaten like asparagus and leaves may be used in tea; young spring shoots can be peeled lightly and cooked while still tender.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional soothing tea herb used for mild digestive and inflammatory complaints.

records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: stem / shoot / stalk, leaf / tea, flower. Best when young. Older stems get tough quickly.

records.notes: Biennial invasive with garlic scent when crushed, scalloped leaves, and white four-petaled flowers.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly culinary here.
records.culinary_uses: Leaves, flowers, and seeds are used as garlicky mustard-flavored greens and seasonings.
records.edibility_detail: Edible and often worth using because it is invasive.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Leaves / Greens, Flowers, Nuts / Seeds.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: leaf / green, flower, seed / nut / cone. Edible and often worth using because it is invasive.

records.notes: Tall yellow composite of meadows, roadsides, and openings. The app keeps this as a broad goldenrod entry focused on tea and medicinal use.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional urinary-support and seasonal-use claims exist; this guide treats those as traditional medicinal context, not medical advice.
records.culinary_uses: Flowers and leaves may be used as an aromatic, slightly bitter tea rather than as a bulk food.
records.edibility_detail: Culinary tea/flavor use is the food-route angle. Therapeutic or urinary-support use belongs to traditional medicinal context.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Flowers, Tea / Infusions.
records.plant_card_note: Flowers and leaves may be used as an aromatic, slightly bitter tea. Traditional medicinal uses exist but are not medical advice.

records.common_name: Goosefoot | close cousin of lamb's quarters
records.notes: Broad goosefoot-group compatibility entry kept because the old app treated some of these as close practical relatives rather than hard species lines.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food weed bucket.
records.culinary_uses: Used much like lamb’s quarters as a cooked green when young and tender.
records.edibility_detail: Treat like the lamb’s quarters lane: young greens are the main use.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Leaves / Greens.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: leaf / green. Treat like the lamb’s quarters lane: young greens are the main use.

records.notes: Lantern-husk fruits on a nightshade-family plant. Only ripe fruit in papery husks is the practical food use.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a fruit entry.
records.culinary_uses: Ripe husked fruits are sweet-tart and used fresh, in jam, pies, salsa, and drying.
records.edibility_detail: Eat only fully ripe fruit; green fruit and many other plant parts are not the same story.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits.

records.notes: Tall sunflower relative with rough leaves, yellow flowers, and edible underground tubers.
records.medicinal_uses: Primarily a food tuber.
records.culinary_uses: Tubers are eaten raw or cooked and have a crisp, sweet, nutty quality.
records.edibility_detail: Edible tubers, often best harvested after frost or in cool seasons.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Roots / Tubers, Flowers.

records.notes: Moist-ground annual with watery stems, alternate toothed leaves, orange spotted pouch-shaped flowers, and spring-loaded seed capsules.
records.culinary_uses: Not treated as a food plant in this guide.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional topical-use plant for skin irritation/itching context; this guide does not treat it as an edible plant.

edible_use.notes: Traditional topical-use plant; not treated as food in this guide.

records.other_uses: Traditional topical use for skin irritation/itching context; not treated as food in this guide.
records.practical_uses: Topical / other-use plant in this guide.

records.notes: Upright annual of disturbed soils with alternate variable leaves and a whitish/mealy coating on young growth and undersides. Green clustered flowers/seed heads appear later.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food weed here.
records.culinary_uses: Young leaves and tender shoots can be cooked like spinach. Use young growth, avoid sprayed/roadside/contaminated sites, and treat seeds as non-beginner processing unless separately researched.
records.general_notes: Practical group-style record for lamb’s quarters / white goosefoot in local field use. Do not identify from “green weed” alone; look for the mealy coating and goosefoot-shaped leaves.
records.food_quality: Good cooked green with moderation/site caution
records.edibility_detail: Edible young greens, preferably cooked. Moderate use is sensible because goosefoots can contain oxalates/nitrates depending site and age.
records.plant_lane_note: Leaves / Greens for young cooked tops; Nuts / Seeds only as a non-beginner seed/processing context.
records.plant_card_note: Common cooked green; look for mealy young leaves and avoid contaminated sites.

use_links.label: Go Botany: Chenopodium album
use_links.notes: Supports the Chenopodium album group context used by this common cooked-green record.

records.notes: Opposite toothed leaves, upright stems, stinging hairs, and greenish flower clusters. Common in moist rich soils, edges, stream bottoms, and disturbed fertile areas.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional tonic plant with urinary, nutritive, and general restorative use context; seeds also have small-quantity tonic use.
records.culinary_uses: Young tops are a useful cooked green once cooked, blanched, dried, or otherwise processed; do not eat raw because the stinging hairs can irritate skin and mouth.
records.general_notes: Group-style record for stinging nettles in the U.P./Great Lakes context; North American taxonomy may be treated as Urtica dioica subsp. gracilis or Urtica gracilis.
records.food_quality: Useful cooked spring green with sting/handling caution
records.edibility_detail: Edible with handling caution. Harvest with gloves; cooking, blanching, or drying neutralizes the sting.
records.plant_lane_note: Young cooked greens; handle with gloves and cook, blanch, or dry to neutralize the sting.
records.plant_card_note: Wear gloves. Do not eat raw stinging nettle; cooking, blanching, or drying neutralizes the stinging hairs.

use_links.label: NC State Extension: Urtica dioica
use_links.notes: Supports stinging hairs/contact caution and cooking young shoots/greens.

records.habitat: Moist rich woods, stream edges, shaded drainages, and damp woodland soils.
records.taste: Cooked green; handle as a stinging plant before cooking.
records.culinary_uses: Young leaves and tender tops can be cooked like nettles after careful handling. Do not eat raw; cooking neutralizes the sting.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional-use plant; not a substitute for medical care.
records.edibility_detail: Edible with handling caution. Stinging hairs can irritate skin; harvest with gloves and cook thoroughly.
records.notes: Similar in use to stinging nettle, but wood nettle has alternate leaves rather than the opposite leaves typical of stinging nettle.
records.general_notes: Added to resolve nettle look-alike references; keep separate from stinging nettle because the leaf arrangement is different.
records.plant_lane_note: Leaves / Greens only; cook before eating and handle with gloves.
records.plant_card_note: Alternate-leaved woodland nettle; use gloves and cook before eating.

use_links.label: Go Botany: Laportea canadensis
use_links.notes: Supports wood nettle identification, habitat, and stinging-hair caution; contrasts wood nettle with stinging nettle.

records.source_note: Wikipedia source replaced in v4.3.147 with Go Botany and USDA PLANTS support; edible-use wording remains conservative: gloves and cooked use only.

records.notes: Low perennial lawn and path weed with a basal rosette of broad oval leaves, strong parallel veins, and leafless flower/seed spikes.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional soothing/topical leaf use exists; in this guide it is mainly tracked as an edible green and tea plant.
records.culinary_uses: Young tender leaves can be eaten raw or cooked; older leaves get fibrous and are better cooked or used as tea.
records.plant_lane_note: Young leaves are the main edible part; dried leaves can be used for tea, and seed use is secondary.
records.plant_card_note: Young leaves are the practical edible part; older leaves become fibrous but can be cooked or used for tea. Look for the basal rosette and strong parallel veins.
records.edibility_detail: Best as young leaves from clean, unsprayed ground. Older leaves are tougher and stringier.

records.notes: Low sprawling succulent with reddish stems and fleshy leaves, common in disturbed sunny ground.
records.medicinal_uses: Some traditional use, but mainly a food green.
records.culinary_uses: Succulent stems and leaves are used raw or cooked in salads, sautés, and pickles.
records.edibility_detail: Edible and crisp, especially when young and fresh.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Leaves / Greens.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: leaf / green, stem / shoot / stalk. Edible and crisp, especially when young and fresh.

records.source_note: Source links added for identification/range context; food-use wording remains modest and caution-framed where appropriate.

records.notes: Spring ephemeral wild onion of rich deciduous woods, often in colonies. Broad leaves emerge early, then die back as the plant flowers and goes dormant.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food allium in this guide.
records.culinary_uses: Leaves and bulbs are edible and strongly onion/garlic flavored, but harvest should be conservative; leaf-only harvest is less damaging than digging bulbs.
records.plant_lane_note: Leaves and bulbs are useful, but the leaves are the more sustainable practical harvest.
records.plant_card_note: Spring wild onion of rich woods; leaves and bulbs are edible, but leaf-only harvest is the better conservation habit.
records.edibility_detail: Leaves and bulbs are edible. Sustainability matters: avoid stripping patches, and harvest lightly if at all.

records.notes: Small sour dock with narrow arrow-like leaves, common in poor soils and open disturbed places.
records.medicinal_uses: Minor medicinal history; mostly an edible tart green here.
records.culinary_uses: Tangy leaves used raw or cooked in small amounts for salads, soups, and sauces.
records.edibility_detail: Edible in small amounts; tartness is the point.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Leaves / Greens.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: leaf / green. Edible in small amounts; tartness is the point.

records.notes: Woodland carrot-family plant with ferny leaves and sweet licorice-like scent.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional aromatic herb.
records.culinary_uses: Leaves, seeds, and roots can be used as sweet aromatic seasoning or tea ingredient.
records.edibility_detail: Edible, but carrot-family caution applies: identification should be solid.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Leaves / Greens, Roots / Tubers, Nuts / Seeds, Tea / Infusions.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: leaf / green, root / tuber / bulb, seed / nut / cone. Edible, but carrot-family caution applies: identification should be solid.

records.look_alike_notes: Sweet cicely is a woodland Apiaceae; licorice-like aroma helps, but smell is not enough by itself. Separate it from deadly hemlocks with stem surface, blotching, habitat, leaf shape, and full plant confirmation. Also separate from wild parsnip, which has yellow-green umbels and sap that can cause sunlight-triggered burns.
records.risk_reason: Sweet cicely has edible/aromatic uses, but carrot-family look-alike risk is serious because white umbels and divided leaves can overlap with deadly species.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Ferny leaves, white umbels, and root/leaf use can tempt shortcuts in a plant family that includes deadly hemlocks.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat sweet cicely unless the plant is positively identified and the hemlock stop-sign checks are negative.

lookalike_risk.label: Extreme — deadly hemlock confusion possible
lookalike_risk.summary: Sweet cicely requires Apiaceae stop-sign checks. Do not rely on white umbels, ferny leaves, or scent alone.
required_checks: Stem hair vs. smoothness check required.
Purple/red blotch check required.
Habitat moisture check required.
Root/taste checks are not substitutes for full positive identification.
Confirm sweet-cicely woodland form and sweet/anise-like odor as supporting traits only.
Reject smooth purple-spotted stems and wetland root situations.
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: If the plant has poison-hemlock or water-hemlock stop signs, do not eat it as sweet cicely.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Deadly poisonous — nervous-system poisoning and respiratory failure risk.
dangerous_lookalikes.warning: Deadly look-alike: smooth purple-spotted stems and hairless ferny leaves are poison-hemlock warning traits.
target_traits: Woodland sweet-cicely form with sweet/anise-like aroma as a supporting clue.
No smooth purple-spotted poison-hemlock stem.
Identification uses the whole plant, not only divided leaves or white umbels.
lookalike_traits: Smooth, hairless, hollow stem instead of a bristly wild-carrot stem.
Purple or reddish blotches/spots on the stem or leaf stalks.
Glossy ferny divided leaves and white umbrella-like flower clusters.
Mousey, musty, or rank odor when crushed.
Deadly poisonous; can affect the nervous and respiratory systems.
required_checks: Check stem surface and purple/red blotches.
Check overall habitat and plant structure.
Do not rely on white umbels alone.
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: If a carrot-family plant has a smooth purple-spotted stem, do not eat it.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Deadly poisonous — seizures and rapid severe poisoning risk.
dangerous_lookalikes.warning: Deadly wetland look-alike: unknown wetland Apiaceae roots are a hard no.
target_traits: Woodland sweet-cicely habit rather than marsh/ditch/shoreline habitat.
No thick or chambered water-hemlock root crown.
Full plant confirmation before any edible/aromatic use.
lookalike_traits: Wetland, marsh, ditch, shore, or streamside habitat raises the danger level immediately.
White umbels and compound leaves with toothed leaflets can overlap with edible carrot-family plants.
Thick root crown or chambered root structure is an extreme danger sign; roots are especially hazardous.
Do not dig, taste, chew, or smell-test unknown wetland Apiaceae roots.
Deadly poisonous; rapid severe poisoning and seizures are possible.
required_checks: Wetland habitat changes the ID problem immediately.
Do not dig or taste unknown wetland Apiaceae roots.
Reject thick/chambered root crowns.
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Do not dig or taste unknown wetland Apiaceae roots.

records.notes: Spring woodland mustard-family plant with paired or whorled leaves depending on species and white to pale pink flowers. Also called crinkleroot or pepper root.
records.culinary_uses: Leaves and roots/rhizomes have peppery mustard-family food use, raw or cooked, in small quantities.
records.medicinal_uses: Not treated as a medicinal plant in this guide.
records.plant_lane_note: Leaves and roots/rhizomes are the useful parts; flowers help with spring ID.
records.plant_card_note: Peppery spring woodland mustard relative; leaves and roots/rhizomes are the useful parts in small amounts.
records.edibility_detail: Edible as a peppery spring green/root in small amounts. Confirm Cardamine/toothwort traits before using.

records.notes: Low woodland and lawn flowers with heart-shaped leaves and purple, blue, yellow, or white blooms depending on species.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional soothing herb.
records.culinary_uses: Leaves and flowers are used in salads, teas, candies, and mild cooked greens.
records.edibility_detail: Widely edible leaves and flowers, especially young leaves.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Leaves / Greens, Flowers, Tea / Infusions.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: leaf / green, flower. Widely edible leaves and flowers, especially young leaves.

records.notes: Biennial Apiaceae with first-year rosettes, feathery leaves, hairy stems, white umbrella flower clusters, and a carrot-scented taproot. Serious hemlock confusion risk: smooth purple-spotted stems or wetland carrot-family roots are stop signs.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional use exists, but this guide treats it as a caution-heavy root entry only.
records.culinary_uses: First-year taproots are the food-side part, but only with expert-level identification; this is not a beginner nibble.
records.plant_lane_note: Root is the useful part; flowers/leaves are ID clues, not extra food lanes.
records.plant_card_note: Expert-only root entry. First-year roots are the possible use, but deadly hemlock confusion and wild-parsnip sap-burn risk make this a hard caution record, not a beginner plant.
records.edibility_detail: Expert-only Apiaceae root entry. Do not treat Wild carrot / Queen Anne’s lace as a beginner root food. Use only a positively identified first-year Daucus carota root from a dry/open site after poison hemlock, water hemlock, and wild parsnip have been ruled out. White umbels, lacy leaves, or carrot smell are not enough.

records.look_alike_notes: Wild carrot is a dangerous Apiaceae comparison plant, not a casual “free carrot.” Do not rely on white umbels, lacy leaves, or carrot smell. Separate it from poison hemlock, water hemlock, and wild parsnip using stem hair/surface, purple blotching, habitat, root structure, flower color, bracts, and sap-burn risk.
records.risk_reason: Apiaceae confusion can be deadly: wild carrot can be mistaken for poison hemlock or water hemlock, and it can be confused with wild parsnip, whose sap can burn sun-exposed skin.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: White/yellow umbels and divided leaves are shared by several carrot-family plants; smooth purple-spotted stems, wetland roots, and yellow wild-parsnip umbels with burn-causing sap are major danger traits.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat wild carrot unless the plant is positively identified as Daucus carota and poison hemlock, water hemlock, and wild parsnip have been ruled out. Smooth purple-spotted stems, wetland habitat, thick/chambered roots, yellow wild-parsnip umbels, or sap-burn risk mean stop.

lookalike_risk.label: Extreme — deadly hemlock confusion possible
lookalike_risk.summary: Wild carrot has dangerous Apiaceae look-alikes. White umbels and lacy leaves are not enough; stem hair, purple blotches, habitat, bracts, odor, yellow wild-parsnip flowers, sap-burn risk, and root context all matter.
required_checks: Stem hair vs. smoothness check required.
Purple/red blotch check required.
Habitat moisture check required.
Root/taste checks are not substitutes for full positive identification.
Queen Anne’s lace bracts and hairy/bristly stem should be present for wild carrot.
Reject wetland carrot-family plants for root use.
Yellow/greenish umbels and coarse parsnip-like leaves point away from Wild carrot and toward wild parsnip/contact-burn caution.
Avoid bare-handed handling of unknown Apiaceae; wild parsnip sap can burn skin after sunlight exposure.
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Smooth purple-spotted stems, unknown wetland Apiaceae roots, or yellow wild-parsnip umbels/contact-burn signs mean do not eat as Wild carrot.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Deadly poisonous — nervous-system poisoning and respiratory failure risk.
dangerous_lookalikes.warning: Deadly look-alike: smooth purple-spotted stems, hairless foliage, and a mousey/musty odor are stop signs.
target_traits: Hairy or bristly stem; not smooth and hairless.
Lacy, finely divided leaves and a carrot smell from a crushed confirmed first-year root.
White Queen Anne’s lace umbel with visible bracts below it; a dark central flower may be present but is not required.
Usually dry/open disturbed habitat such as fields, roadsides, or dry open ground.
Food use is limited to a positively identified first-year root in a safe context.
lookalike_traits: Smooth, hairless, hollow stem instead of a bristly wild-carrot stem.
Purple or reddish blotches/spots on the stem or leaf stalks.
Glossy ferny divided leaves and white umbrella-like flower clusters.
Mousey, musty, or rank odor when crushed.
Deadly poisonous; can affect the nervous and respiratory systems.
required_checks: Stem must be hairy/bristly for wild carrot.
Reject smooth hairless stems with purple/red blotches.
Do not rely on white umbels alone.
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: If a carrot-family plant has a smooth purple-spotted stem, do not eat it.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Deadly poisonous — rapid nervous-system poisoning, seizures, and respiratory failure risk.
dangerous_lookalikes.warning: Deadly wetland look-alike: wet habitat plus carrot-family white umbels or thick roots is a hard stop.
target_traits: Hairy or bristly stem; not smooth and hairless.
Lacy, finely divided leaves and a carrot smell from a crushed confirmed first-year root.
White Queen Anne’s lace umbel with visible bracts below it; a dark central flower may be present but is not required.
Usually dry/open disturbed habitat such as fields, roadsides, or dry open ground.
Food use is limited to a positively identified first-year root in a safe context.
lookalike_traits: Wetland, marsh, ditch, shore, or streamside habitat raises the danger level immediately.
White umbels and compound leaves with toothed leaflets can overlap with edible carrot-family plants.
Thick root crown or chambered root structure is an extreme danger sign; roots are especially hazardous.
Do not dig, taste, chew, or smell-test unknown wetland Apiaceae roots.
Deadly poisonous; rapid severe poisoning and seizures are possible.
required_checks: Dry/open disturbed habitat supports wild carrot; wetland habitat does not.
Do not dig or taste unknown wetland Apiaceae roots.
Treat chambered or thick root crowns as deadly danger traits.
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Do not dig or taste unknown wetland Apiaceae roots.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Dangerous contact-burn plant; edible root only with serious caution.
dangerous_lookalikes.warning: Dangerous look-alike / caution plant: yellow-green umbels and sap that can cause severe sunlight-triggered burns. The root is edible only with certain ID and protective handling.
target_traits: Wild carrot has white Queen Anne’s lace umbels, fine lacy leaves, and a hairy/bristly stem.
Food use is limited to a positively identified first-year Wild carrot root in a safe dry/open context.
Wild carrot should not have yellow-green wild-parsnip flower clusters.
lookalike_traits: Yellow to yellow-green flat-topped compound umbels rather than white Queen Anne’s lace flowers.
Coarse compound leaves with 5-15 toothed leaflets, not the same fine lacy Wild carrot leaf texture.
Stout hollow grooved stem; plant often appears along sunny roadsides, fields, and disturbed open ground.
Sap from leaves, stems, flowers, and fruit can cause severe photodermatitis after sunlight exposure.
First-year taproot is edible with serious caution, but this does not make the plant casual or beginner-safe.
required_checks: Check flower color: yellow/greenish umbels are not Wild carrot.
Avoid sap contact; wear gloves and long sleeves around suspected wild parsnip.
Do not dig or taste unknown Apiaceae roots.
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Yellow wild-parsnip umbels or sap exposure risk means do not treat the plant as Wild carrot.

records.notes: Low woodland groundcover with paired heart-shaped leaves and hidden brown-purple flowers near the ground. Historical rhizome use exists, but safety concerns make this a questionable food entry.
records.medicinal_uses: Historical medicinal use exists, but internal use is not recommended by this guide because of aristolochic-acid safety concerns.
records.culinary_uses: Historical rhizome flavoring only. This guide does not recommend internal use or food use.
records.plant_lane_note: Removed from edible plant lanes; retained only as a caution/historical-use record.
records.plant_card_note: Historical wild ginger use is noted for context only. Do not use internally; this is not treated as an edible root/tuber in this guide.
records.edibility_detail: Historical use only; internal use is not recommended. Do not use wild ginger rhizomes, leaves, teas, tinctures, or preparations as food or medicine.

records.safety_note: Aristolochic-acid concerns in Aristolochiaceae/Asarum support conservative hard-block wording: historical use only; internal use not recommended.
records.hard_rule: Do not ingest wild ginger or use it as a tea, root substitute, tincture, or medicine.

records.notes: Broad mustard-family greens bucket. Look for peppery young leaves, four-petaled yellow or white flowers, and slender seed pods; avoid sprayed roadsides, farm edges, or polluted sites.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly culinary in this guide.
records.culinary_uses: Young leaves are the main use and are best before the plant gets tough and strongly bitter. Flower buds, flowers, and mature seeds can also be used as peppery seasoning.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Leaves / Greens, Flowers, Nuts / Seeds.
records.plant_card_note: Young peppery leaves are the main use; buds, flowers, and mature seeds can season food. Avoid sprayed or polluted disturbed ground.
records.edibility_detail: Edible with correct mustard-family identification. Older plants can be tough or bitter; avoid contaminated or sprayed sites.
look_alikes: Young non-mustard weeds in disturbed soil

records.notes: Low shamrock-like Oxalis with trifoliate leaves and small delicate flowers. This consolidated record also absorbs the old yellow wood-sorrel part record.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a flavoring green here.
records.culinary_uses: Tangy leaves and flowers are used fresh in small amounts as a sour accent, not as a bulk green.
records.edibility_detail: Edible in modest amounts; tart oxalic-acid flavor is the point, not heavy eating.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Leaves / Greens, Flowers.
records.plant_card_note: Tangy leaves and flowers are useful as a sour accent in small amounts; this is not a bulk green.

records.notes: Aromatic perennial with finely divided feathery leaves and flat-topped clusters of small white to pale flowers.
records.culinary_uses: Young leaves are a bitter aromatic herb in small amounts; flowers and leaves are more commonly used for tea.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional tea and wound/herbal plant; this guide treats it as tea/traditional-use context, not medical advice.

images_structured.credit: Pablo Alberto Salguero Quiles, CC BY-SA 3.0/GFDL, via Wikimedia Commons

records.plant_lane_note: Flowers/leaves are mainly tea/herb parts; leaf use as a green is small-quantity only.
records.plant_card_note: Aromatic bitter leaves and flowers are mostly a tea/herb use, not a bulk food. Keep safety context visible.
records.edibility_detail: Use as a small-quantity bitter herb or tea plant. Avoid heavy use and review safety if pregnant, on medication, or sensitive to Asteraceae plants.

records.commonness: Locally common invasive in suitable disturbed/wet-edge habitats
records.food_quality: Edible young shoots with invasive-spread warning
records.culinary_uses: Young spring shoots can be cooked like a sour rhubarb-like vegetable when harvested from clean, unsprayed sites. Mature stems become tough and are not a food target.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional-use/invasive-plant discussions exist, but this guide keeps medical claims conservative.
records.other_uses: Invasive-plant awareness: learn it so you do not spread it by moving rhizomes, stem fragments, or contaminated soil.
records.edibility_detail: Edible young shoots only. Do not transport fragments, rhizomes, or soil; do not compost pieces casually. Avoid treated/sprayed colonies.
records.notes: Hollow bamboo-like stems with raised nodes, zigzag stems, reddish new growth, broad leaves, dense colonies, and creamy-white late-season flower sprays. The ecological warning is as important as the food use.
records.general_notes: Hollow bamboo-like stems with raised nodes, zigzag stems, reddish new growth, broad leaves, dense colonies, and creamy-white late-season flower sprays. The ecological warning is as important as the food use.
records.plant_lane_note: Leaves / Greens lane for young spring shoots; do not spread fragments, rhizomes, or soil.
records.plant_card_note: Young shoots can be food; the hard rule is do not spread fragments or soil.
